data.title: Aussie Dollar Fatigue? Technical Signs Hint At An AUD/USD Pullback
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

AUD:USD has been on a tear since January 19, rallying some 500-odd pips since. The move has coincided with US dollar weakness and a renewed appetite for emerging markets and commodity-linked currencies like the Australian dollar.

The current rally appears to have found a top around the 0.7150 handle, before ending last week with two bearish days. This has raised the question: Is a deeper correction on its way for AUD/USD?

Fundamental outlook - Central Bank policy divergence

The longer-term fundamental picture still supports further Aussie dollar gains as central bank policy divergence comes into play.

The RBA has raised rates at its recent meeting with the potential for more rate hikes, while the Federal Reserve continues to eye rate cuts at some point this year.

However, in the short term, a pullback still may materialize, and looking at the recent price action, there do appear to be signs to support this narrative.

Just looking at implied rates for the Fed and the RBA, the divergence is evident. According to LSEG data, markets are pricing in around 37 bps of rate hikes for the RBA through December 2026.

RBA implied rates for 2026

Now switching to the Federal Reserve, markets are pricing in around 66 bps of rate cuts after last week's softer-than-expected CPI print. This leaves the Aussie dollar in pole position for more gains as the year progresses.

Federal Reserve implied rates for 2026

Technical Analysis - AUD/USD

From a technical point of view, the best place to start is the weekly chart.

On a weekly time frame, we have seen upper wicks, which may be a sign that bullish momentum is fading.

Couple that with the period-14 RSI, which is in overbought territory.

Are these signs of fatigue?

AUD/USD Weekly Chart, February 16, 2026

Dropping down to a four-hour time frame, and support at 0.70690 is key.

A break of this level, though, still faces significant hurdles with the 50 and 100-day MAs resting just below at 0.7054 and 0.7011 respectively.

A break of these levels as well as the 0.7000 handle could open up a deeper retracement toward the 0.6913 and potentially the 200-day MA at 0.6861.

This would be considered the safety play for market participants.

The more aggressive traders may look at any move higher toward the recent highs as a potential trade opportunity.

Stops would need to go just above the recent highs with a bit of breathing room in the event of any spikes, around the 0.7170 handle.

Such a move will present a better risk-to-reward opportunity but is also a higher-risk trade setup.

AUD/USD Four-Hour Chart, February 16, 2026

Client sentiment data - AUD/USD

Looking at OANDA client sentiment data, market participants are short on AUD/USD, with 59% of traders net-short. I prefer to take a contrarian view toward crowd sentiment, and thus, the fact that so many traders are short means AUD/USD could rise in the near term before a potential selloff.

Looking ahead at potential catalysts for AUD/USD

This week will bring both the RBA and Federal Reserve meeting minutes, which will shed further light on the monetary policy positions moving forward. These events could stoke significant volatility in AUD/USD.

From a data perspective, we will also get the Australian employment change data. The US has a few high-impact data releases ahead, with PCE, GDP and PMI data all ahead before the end of the week.

data.title: Hartford Capital Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities advanced in the fourth quarter, registering a sizable return for 2025. Markets were bolstered by robust corporate earnings, resilient consumer spending, and solid economic growth, although anxiety about lofty valuations in the technology sector and concerns surrounding massive AI spending drove capital toward value/cyclical areas of the market. The cooling labor market and worries about upside inflation risks also kept the markets on edge.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Capital Appreciation Fund (I Share)(ITHIX) underperformed the Russell 3000 Index during the quarter.
  • Sector allocation, a result of the portfolio’s bottom-up stock-selection process, modestly detracted from relative results. The negative effects from our underweight exposure to healthcare and overweight exposure to consumer discretionary were only partially offset by the positive effects from our overweight exposure to information technology and underweight exposure to industrials.
  • Security selection modestly benefitted relative performance over the period. Selection within the communication services and financials sectors was a tailwind to relative performance, while selection within the information technology sector was a headwind.
  • The impact from style was negative this quarter, driven by the Fund’s lower exposure in high-momentum names. This was only partially offset by the Fund’s slight underweight exposure to names with heightened volatility and its beta positioning, which contributed.
  • The top relative detractors from performance during the period included a lack of exposure to Micron Technology (MU)(information technology) and an out-of-benchmark position in ARM (information technology). The top relative contributors to performance during the quarter included the Fund’s overweight positions in Alphabet (GOOGL)(communication services) and Eli Lilly (LLY)(healthcare).

Positioning & Outlook

  • Equities extended their gains through the end of 2025, yet we remain focused on managing potential risks amid uncertainty around central-bank policy, trade relations, the possibility of an AI-driven bubble, and ongoing geopolitical tensions. We believe these dynamics could introduce heightened market volatility while simultaneously creating opportunities for skilled fundamental stock pickers adept at managing through uncertain market environments. As always, risk management remains at the forefront of our investment process as we seek to structurally allocate across managers and styles to create a well-diversified portfolio.
  • At the end of the period, the largest overweights were to the consumer-discretionary and information technology sectors, while the largest underweights were to the healthcare and industrials sectors, relative to the benchmark.

data.title: Micron: Nothing Is Over
data.description:
I Believe Micron's Rally Should Keep Going

Momentum-wise, Micron Technology (MU) stock turned out to be one of the strongest semiconductor stocks among mid-caps (and large caps), with a lot of people now wondering when this super cycle ends and when they should take profits. I was premature in September 2025 when I downgraded to "Hold," and I then admitted my excessive pessimism in early January. MU has kept climbing higher since then, getting closer to my updated price target:

And I still believe MU's bullish rally hasn't played out in full just yet. Micron is valued at around a $460 billion market cap, yet it's going to report almost $100 billion in revenues in FY2027 alone, with a margin level it probably hasn't seen in its corporate history. It might look like a typical cycle peak, but it's not, given the demand/supply dynamics in the memory chips niche these days. Assuming the cycle isn't peaking yet, I'm leaving my "Buy" on Micron with no changes.

Why Do I Think So?

In MU's fiscal Q1 2026, we saw $13.64 billion in revenues (+57% YoY), well above the management's initial high-end guidance (and, of course, well above the consensus as well). The non-GAAP gross margin was at 56.8%, and it was 17.3% above last year's figure - the level of Micron's pricing power makes it look like a fabless logic leader rather than a memory manufacturer. The market environment is extremely bullish, and it's not reversing at this point, promising even stronger pricing power ahead for Micron:

Based on the December release, Micron was going to exit the Crucial consumer retail business, effectively ending sales of branded SSDs and DRAM through retail and e-tail channels by February 2026. This should have helped them redirect every available wafer toward AI customers, and these customers bring in higher margins for Micron, so I think amid the above pricing dynamics, the positive earnings revisions that we've seen over the past 3-6 months aren't excessive at all.

Moreover, the lack of EPS estimates changes over the past 1 month - given that the revenue projections were adjusted higher - looks too pessimistic to me.

I think Micron is very likely to preserve its operating leverage (if not increase it) over the next couple of years amid the current pricing trends, and so the lack of EPS revisions points to a possible series of strong bullish beats throughout calendar 2026 and maybe even 2027. Why am I including 2027 here? It's simply because there's no clarity yet on when exactly the memory chip shortage will finally fade. Based on Sassine Ghazi's commentaries (CEO of Synopsys), the chip crunch will continue through 2026-2027:

The likely revisions repricing that I've just described above is a catalyst that might appear over and over again as Micron reports fresh earnings each quarter.

What makes this cycle different is the specifics of the end consumers. Data centers should reportedly consume about 70% of memory chips made in 2026, which is a huge structural shift in the customer structure for Micron and its Korean peers (Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix). And 2026 isn't going to be the peak year in terms of data center build-outs - according to JLL's research, the global data center sector should expand at a 14% CAGR through 2030 amid the rise in inference's role. This is going to add a lot to the existing infrastructure investment supercycle, making it last longer, and hence making Micron's earnings generation more sustainable.

The usual path of the memory chip cycle is when higher demand leads to higher supply (producers start to scale to justify the rising needs). And when the demand weakens eventually, the oversupply hits the market and leads to the pricing reversal and hence earnings erosion. But now, Micron and its peers operate amid technical and physical constraints - DRAM scaling has become exponentially harder:

In prior cycles DRAM density could improve by about 100 times a decade, but in the last decade DRAM density has only increased by 2 times. Suppliers could also quickly increase supply through node shrinks, and while still possible, each node shrink is now more expensive, slower, more complex, and adds less supply than it used to. There are also new technical challenges like tiny capacitor charge storage, extreme aspect ratio structures, and signal-to-noise constraints. Thus, while previous cycle supply was constrained primarily by capital, this cycle is also constrained by technical limits. In other words, the massively increased scaling difficulty is a major reason I think memory is no longer cyclical.

Source: Seeking Alpha, ML Research's article

In other words, even if we consider MU's massive CapEx ahead, new bit supply is going to be increasingly limited, thus keeping the market in a state of structural undersupply and effectively postponing even the equilibrium for the next couple of years.

The rise in demand and structural undersupply is making Micron's TAM expand more rapidly than we could have imagined before. The company projects that HBM TAM will grow at roughly a 40% CAGR (to around $100 billion by 2028), which means a key milestone is now expected to arrive about two years earlier than prior projections. The HBM in general is going to be larger than the entire DRAM market was in 2024. And with the transition from Nvidia's (NVDA) H100 to the B300, will involve a 3.5x increase in HBM content per chip, Micron's backlog is likely to enjoy constant additions, in my opinion. When Micron gets the first DRAM outputs from the Idaho fab in mid-2027 and then launches the 2nd fab in 2028, Micron's US footprint will become a strategic moat for them, with or without Lutnick's potential 100% tariffs on foreign memory chipmakers.

Okay, so if we assume that there's no cycle peak right now and that the potential peak comes after 2028, we can discuss Micron's valuation.

MU is trading at ~9.5x FY2027 earnings, which is a PEG of ~0.33 if the current consensus is right:

What we can consider as close-to-being-true projections are FY2026-2027 because we have a sufficient number of analysts forming the consensus. The last time I looked at MU's EPS projections, I saw FY2027 EPS at $39.37, and now it's at $43.39. My 10% premium that I was putting in my model back then has already been realized. I think the trajectory in pricing suggests that we'll see another 10-15% boost to the FY2027 EPS consensus throughout 2026. So, if my previous P/E target of 11.5x stays unchanged, I'll arrive at a price target of $561.36 per share, which is over 27% above the current stock price.

$43.39 * 1.125 * 11.5x = $561.36/share

All this makes me stay bullish on Micron in the medium term.

Risks

Just like last time in January, my price target on Micron comes pretty close to the most bullish Wall Street PT suggests. The difference is that my PT is >7% higher than the high end.

I might fail to consider some risks or future events that might abruptly stop Micron's rally at some point this year. As I noted in January, and this concern still stays valid for MU, in case demand for the firm's offerings stalls, the memory pricing can turn quickly, which is going to hurt the company's margins and lead to a bearish repricing of the stock price. Competition in the niche is also a major risk factor because we know that Samsung and SK Hynix have great tech capabilities that could flood the market and hurt MU's market share.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks and the fact that Micron has already quadrupled over the past year, I think nothing is over for buyers. Usually, when the cycle is peaking, we see low multiples, which point to a likely correction, not continuation. But this time, the cycle isn't flashing any major red signs. The supply of memory chips is here to stay for years, and Micron's expansion plans in the US position the firm well to keep increasing its earnings at a rapid pace. I think the beating potential hasn't gone anywhere - what the current consensus expects for FY2028 and beyond doesn't look logical to me (it's simply too pessimistic given the market's setup). So, I expect more repricing to the upside. My updated valuation model sees another 27% upside in the next 12 months, so I keep my "Buy" untouched.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: EUREP Expansion And The Euro - Going Global
data.description:
By Chris Turner, Global Head of Markets and Regional Head of Research for UK & CEE, Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist and Dmitry Dolgin, Chief Economist, CIS

Q: What has been announced?

Following reports from a week earlier, the European Central Bank announced over the weekend that, starting in the third quarter of 2026, it will substantially expand its EUREP euro repo line for central banks. This facility was originally established at the height of the pandemic to provide euro liquidity to eight non-euro area European central banks, including those in Hungary and Romania. This facility is now going global; any central bank is invited to apply to set up a line if it isn't excluded on the grounds of AML or international sanctions.

In exchange for high-quality euro collateral (issued by central, regional or local governments), the facility allows foreign central banks access to euro liquidity at a ‘backstop rate’ (higher than market) and is to be used in adverse conditions. The announcement has primarily been grounded in the financial stability concerns of a geo-fragmenting global economy. The news was also timed to coincide with Europe’s Munich Security Conference.

This facility now looks akin to the Federal Reserve's Foreign and International Monetary Authority (FIMA) repo facility, which was established in 2022 and allows foreign central banks to raise dollar liquidity by repo-ing and not selling US Treasuries. The ECB wants the same to occur here; if there are any squeezes on access to euro funding, foreign central banks can tap into these very generous EUREP lines (€50bn) to access euros as opposed to selling euro-denominated paper.

Use of EUREP has been modest and sporadic since 2020 (EUR bn)

Q: What does it mean for European Government Bonds?

In theory, this should make central bank FX reserve managers more comfortable in holding European government bond paper – knowing that it can be used to raise euro liquidity in extreme circumstances and avoid the fire sale of eurozone assets that occurred during the eurozone crisis. The collateral criteria allow for euro-denominated, investment grade (BBB-rated or higher) EEA central or local/regional government (€11.6tr outstanding), as well as recognised agency and supranational debt securities to be pledged. While ratings below single-A do translate into a higher haircut schedule, the ECB employs a generous first-best rule across five rating agencies (Fitch, Moody’s, S&P, DBRS and Scope). This means that, within EGBs, currently only Greek bonds see the higher haircuts in practice.

Q: What are the broader implications for use of the euro?

The EUREP is a repo facility that provides euro liquidity in exchange for euro collateral and, as such, is different to an FX swap line, where the ECB accepts good-quality FX in exchange for euro liquidity. At present, the ECB has FX swap arrangements with the likes of the Fed, the Bank of England, the Bank of Japan, the Swiss National Bank, the Riksbank and the People's Bank of China.

But we think the roll-out of this broader EUREP facility could have the ancillary benefits of FX swap line roll-outs, which countries like China have been pursuing to enhance the use of the renminbi in international trade. Presumably, FX swaps are more accessible, but a euro-denominated facility would be supported by the already deep bond market and large international footing; there are €11.5tr of EUR-denominated international debt securities outstanding, which is 40.5% of the global market.

China has been expanding the PBoC’s bilateral FX swap-line network, which functions primarily as an emergency liquidity channel. The bilateral agreement network now covers over 40 countries, with around 35 of those agreements established before 2016, although new lines still appear almost every one to two years. This expansion has supported China’s efforts to deepen its international trade and financial ties and advance CNY's international role: between 2021 and 2025, the CNY share in SWIFT trade-finance transactions more than quadrupled to 8.3%, overtaking the euro for second place worldwide. At the same time, China’s RMB payment system, CIPS, has grown rapidly in geography and volume, reaching RMB700-800bn ($100-115bn) in daily transaction volume.

Greater international comfort in holding the euro in FX reserves should – especially given that 40% of international debt securities are already denominated in euros – support greater use of the euro in trade invoicing and reinforce our view of a gradual transition from a unipolar dollar world to a multipolar world of dollars, euros and renminbi.

Currency share in trade finance

Q: Does this tell us anything about the ECB’s tolerance of a strong euro?

Debate is growing about whether the ECB’s desire for a strong international euro means it is prepared to accept a strong nominal euro as well. The official take will be what the appreciation of the nominal trade-weighted euro means for the ECB inflation forecast and whether inflation undershooting would prompt rate cuts. Our take is that it would require a move much closer to 1.25 in EUR/USD before that conversation grows serious.

However, the current geopolitical environment seems to have prioritised the need for a global euro. And we certainly seem a very long way from the period in the mid-2000s when the euro’s role as a reserve currency seemed problematic for the ECB. That said, even at these levels, we are hearing today that the French government wants a further economic assessment of whether promoting the use of the euro will push up EUR/USD and hurt French exporters.

Our current baseline sees EUR/USD ending the year at 1.22 – with upside risks.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Airbnb: Upgrading To Buy Amid Growth Acceleration
data.description:
More than two years have passed since I downgraded Airbnb, Inc. (ABNB) to a hold rating in February 2024. ABNB stock has lost ~18% of its value since then. In hindsight, I feel a downgrade to sell would have been more prudent. That said, as a long-term-oriented investor, I am quite willing to weather short-term volatility in stock prices as long as the long-term outlook remains bright for a business that I love. Airbnb most certainly met this criterion back in February 2024.

On February 13, Airbnb stock gained ~5% after reporting Q4 2025 earnings. After digesting the earnings print and reading the earnings call transcript, I have found a few data points to suggest that Airbnb is turning a corner. 2026 is shaping up to be a year in which Airbnb is likely to enjoy a meaningful acceleration in growth.

I am turning bullish on ABNB as I believe the company's growth acceleration story deserves a valuation re-rating.

Project Y is Delivering the Goods

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Airbnb reported revenue of $2.8 billion, a YoY increase of 12%. This was an acceleration from the ~10% average growth the company reported in the past 4 quarters. The company's guidance for 14% to 16% revenue growth in Q1 2026 represents a major acceleration in growth compared to the TTM average as well.

Net income was down 30% YoY to $341 million. Although alarming, I do not believe this decline should give headaches to long-term investors, as it was driven by one-time tax items totaling $90 million and strategic growth investments. The core business remained profitable, evident from the 3% YoY growth in adjusted EBITDA to $786 million. Growth investments that exerted pressure on net income proved to be a drag on EBITDA margins as well (down from 31% a year ago to 28% in Q4 2025). The company reported $521 million in free cash flows in Q4, bringing the full-year FCF to $4.6 billion.

Revenue growth was driven by a strong 16% YoY growth in GBV to $20.4 billion. Nights and Seats Booked grew 10% YoY to almost 122 million as well.

Exhibit 1: Airbnb Q4 2025 and Full-year Performance

Project Y, Airbnb's strategic initiative to accelerate growth through operational efficiencies and product innovation, contributed to the acceleration of growth in Q4. Commenting on how Project Y boosted revenue in 2025, CEO Brian Chesky revealed that some of these initiatives contributed "hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue".

Going a step further to discuss which initiatives drove the biggest gains for Airbnb, CEO Chesky revealed that the reserve now, pay later feature, new cancellation updates, and the simplification of booking fees rolled out as part of Project Y contributed ~200 basis points of growth to Nights Booked and ~300 basis points to GBV.

Among these new initiatives, I am most impressed by the very high early adoption rates for the reserve now, pay later feature, which allows travelers to book eligible stays with no payment upfront. From a psychological perspective, this feature incentivizes travelers to choose larger, more expensive accommodation options than if they were to pay for a booking up front.

After successfully implementing many of the Project Y initiatives in the domestic market, Airbnb began expanding this blueprint to international markets in Q4. Brazil was one of the markets that materially benefited from these initiatives, as the company's decision to create a specialized, dedicated team to localize the Airbnb experience boosted customer engagement with the platform.

Moving forward, as the Project Y model is implemented across its key global markets, I believe Airbnb is likely to see a major improvement in margins and also a meaningful revenue boost.

Fears of Pricing Pressure Are Overblown

When I downgraded Airbnb to a hold rating in 2024, I was among many investors who feared pricing pressures. While these fears proved to be well-grounded in the first few quarters following my rating downgrade, the company's Q4 performance suggests the narrative has changed dramatically today. One of the biggest fears I had was an oversupply in the travel rental market driving ADRs lower. The opposite happened in Q4. ADR grew 6% YoY to $168 in Q4, recording an acceleration from 1% and 3% YoY growth reported in Q2 and Q3, respectively. Even for the first quarter of 2026, the management guided for a modest increase in ADRs, which is a strong indicator of structural ADR growth as opposed to a one-time benefit resulting from holiday demand in Q4.

AI Investments Will Boost Growth and Operating Efficiency

Airbnb is investing in AI to accelerate revenue growth and improve operating margins in the long run. For now, we may not see the positive impact on operating margins as the company is still aggressively investing in new products and growth initiatives. Investors should ideally focus on the long-term outlook.

Airbnb poached Ahmad Al-Dahle to fill the CTO position last January. Mr. Al-Dahle is considered a global AI leader. He spent many years at Meta Platforms (META), where he led the tech giant's generative AI team. Airbnb wants to become an AI-native company, and Al-Dahle's appointment comes at this crucial time. I believe this move could accelerate Airbnb's AI adoption in the foreseeable future, possibly helping the company emerge as the leader of the pack for AI integration among its peers. According to the management, the end goal is not just to use existing generative AI models to improve operational efficiency but to transform the user experience by building a specialized AI stack that can address the pitfalls of using generic AI models. One of the biggest changes that could transform the way travelers book accommodation is the introduction of visual travel booking assistants.

Exhibit 2: Benefits of AI in travel

At a broad level, integration of AI has already helped Airbnb boost its operational efficiency through more efficient customer service. According to company filings, in North America, AI agents are currently resolving a third of all support issues without involving a human agent. This leads to faster case resolution for customers and lower operating costs for Airbnb. As part of future development plans, Airbnb is targeting the rollout of a voice AI assistant, which would be a major upgrade that allows customers to talk to an AI agent rather than texting with an AI agent to resolve issues even faster. Furthermore, a global rollout of AI assistant features is planned for this year.

Although these efforts are likely to boost margins in the long run, we might not see any meaningful improvement in the next few quarters. This is because of Airbnb's aggressive investments. CFO Ellie Mertz said:

Across the full P&L, we're continuing to drive efficiencies in our platform. We plan to reinvest most of these efficiencies into marketing, product and technology to support our growth. As a result, we expect our 2026 adjusted EBITDA margin to be stable year-over-year.

Airbnb's AI plans are not just about margin expansion. Under the new CTO, the company plans to roll out advanced AI search features and personalization options to make the booking process smoother and more interactive. I believe these initiatives will have a direct impact on revenue growth in the long run.

Risks

The outlook for the travel industry remains optimistic, but there is a real risk of consumers turning more value-conscious this year, which might prove to be a challenge for Airbnb at a time when it is investing aggressively. The company's pricing power will take a hit in such a macro environment. I am keeping an eye on geopolitical tensions as well. In addition to a potential escalation in tensions in the Middle East, the U.S. may end up enforcing stricter visa rules, threatening the growth of international tourists. Regulatory risks need to be monitored too, especially in Europe, where policymakers have been pushing for stricter regulations for AI pricing tools. Airbnb's AI investments may come under scrutiny this year in this case.

Takeaway

Airbnb's growth is accelerating. More than a macro improvement, the acceleration in growth is driven by company-specific developments, such as the success of Project Y initiatives and the integration of AI. The growth in ADR in Q4 2025 suggests fears of pricing pressures are overblown too. I believe the outlook for Airbnb has shifted materially enough to warrant a re-rating. After more than 2 years, I am bullish on the prospects for ABNB, as I believe the company has positioned itself to enjoy an acceleration in revenue and earnings growth even if macro conditions remain the same.

data.title: IREN Investors: Look Beyond That $3.4B Target
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last week, upcoming neocloud provider IREN Ltd. (NASDAQ:IREN) reiterated its year-end target in its Q2 FY26 ER to achieve $3.4B in ARR as it continues to build out GPU capacity that enables it to achieve those ARR targets.

In the current investing climate prevalent for growth stocks, a mere reiteration of a prior target will result in shares getting chastised. This is what happened to IREN’s shares post-Q2 ER, leading to the neocloud company’s shares dropping 22% last week.

However, if investors cut through the post-ER pessimism, they will find enough bullish catalysts that are firmly positioning IREN to accelerate its AI services revenues further. Per my analysis, IREN is making significant advancements in three key areas—power, GPU deployments, and financing—to accelerate the neocloud business momentum it has become a beneficiary of.

The current drawdown makes IREN meaningfully attractive again, and I reiterate my bullishness on the neocloud’s prospects for this year.

Buy Any Pullback - IREN’s Buildout Will Accelerate Growth

In September last year, I wrote, “IREN timed its AI pivot to perfection,” a thesis that is playing out in its full entirety as the price of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) collapses, impacting the economics of mining Bitcoin.

For IREN, the timing of that AI pivot was extremely evident in their Q2 FY26 results, which showed just how the two business lines were diverging, not just in growth but also in predictability of forward revenue growth.

In Q2, IREN reported total revenues worth $184.7M, up 60%. That 60% pace was only possible because AI cloud services revenues surged 137% sequentially, or a mammoth 541% y/y, to $17.3M, offsetting the Bitcoin mining revenue that moved in the other direction, up 48% y/y but down 28.2% sequentially to $167.4M.

The thorn in IREN’s Q2 ER was the lack of upgrades to its revenue targets. On the call to discuss Q2 earnings, IREN’s management said:

We expect to deliver 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026, positioning us to deliver $3.4 billion in annualized run rate revenue.

This directly signals a reiteration of the $3.4B ARR target that was announced on the Q1 FY26 call, which is something markets did not like. Also, IREN signaled that they are on track to deploy 140k GPUs by the end of this year, a GPU deployment target that was already revealed by IREN on the Q1 call. This is one of the key reasons why IREN’s shares sold off post the Q2 ER due to the lack of meaningful growth catalysts that markets were looking for.

If markets look past these two numbers, they find a goldmine of forward-looking projections that can easily build the bullish case for a strong 2026. Management did divulge plans in 3 key areas of their operating framework that robustly position IREN to be a strong neocloud beneficiary of the AI infrastructure revolution.

First, is the contracted power secured? IREN increased the volume of contracted power it secured during the quarter by adding an additional 1.6 GW of contracted power by securing a site in Oklahoma. This reflects in the amount of growth runway IREN is building for itself. To explain this from the point of view with a deeper impact, management explained:

Our $3.4 billion ARR target for the end of calendar 2026 reflects utilization of only around 10% of our 4.5 gigawatts of secured grid-connected power capacity. That means the vast majority of our portfolio remains available to support additional deployments. With demand continuing to build, that secured capacity gives us the ability to keep engaging customers on new large-scale opportunities and to extend growth well beyond the 2026 target.

Securing that much contracted power ahead of time helps IREN deploy resources to build out capacity on that contracted power grid as and when IREN signs new deals and wins new contracts. IREN also revealed plans for how quickly they are bringing online GPU capacity from this secured power grid, bringing me to point #2.

Second—volume of capacity brought online/energized. While IREN brought online ~810 MW of GPU capacity in Q2, they are on track to energize 1.4 GW of capacity in the next 3-4 months. The bulk of that forward capacity that is expected to be energized will be at IREN’s Sweetwater 1 location in Texas. That’s very positive in my view and signals that they might already be engaging with either a new hyperscaler customer or with Microsoft (MSFT) to expand their previous $9.7B five-year deal.

And third—financing and capital arrangements. Management revealed that it had already secured $3.6 billion in GPU financing at less than 6% interest, an attractive rate in today’s elevated interest rate environment. Combined with $1.9 billion in customer prepayments, this covers 95% of CapEx for their $9.7 billion Microsoft contract.

Taken together, what these three points tell me is that IREN’s management is successfully using its forward commitments from the Microsoft deal to secure financing at attractive rates to enable the company to not only secure large volumes of power capacity but also deploy resources swiftly to energize much of this power capacity and bring GPUs online faster. The end result that I see is two-fold. One, IREN wins the ability to recognize revenue from its Microsoft contract faster than expected. Two, IREN also dangles the promise of secured power and energized capacity to lure more customers who are hungry to secure more GPU capacity for their own model training/inferencing needs.

This is something markets, at the current point in time, are not recognizing, which is creating attractive entry points for IREN again, like I called out in September last year.

IREN’s Valuation Could Compress Further - But Upside Is Huge

There are two main drivers of what will move the needle for IREN’s multiple.

The first is a new ARR that IREN scores by winning new contracts, either from its current customers or from winning new customers entirely. That would push up its ARR target of $3.4B, which was probably what markets were expecting in the first place last week.

The second is if IREN surprises markets with strong revenue beats, which can only come as a result of faster-than-expected deployments of GPU capacity resulting in a quicker-than-expected pace of recognizing revenue.

Either way, IREN has enough room to win newer customer contracts, and I fully expect IREN to deliver newer wins, which could push the needle on both drivers above. That means IREN has a clear path towards being valued at ~20x forward revenues, given that its revenues are expected to accelerate every single quarter this year, with the December quarter expected to demonstrate a 290% jump in revenues to $721M.

In the short term, pessimism might persist, putting pressure on IREN’s forward revenue multiple, pushing it down to ~10x revenues. But if investors maintain a year-long horizon, any pullbacks will be strong opportunities to load up on IREN.

To summarize, short-term pessimism may push IREN’s forward revenue multiple to 10x, implying ~20% downside, but I expect IREN’s strong growth plans to eventually be rewarded by markets, leading to expansion in the forward revenue multiple to 20x, implying ~53% upside.

Risks & Other Factors To Know

As mentioned above, over the next few months, IREN’s forward revenue multiple may face pressure due to the lack of updates to its $3.4B ARR target. But I do expect the outlook to improve as management’s action plan signals strong runways of growth this year. That’s based on the backdrop that hyperscalers continue to portion their capex towards neoclouds like IREN, who carry the execution risk of building out GPU capacity for hyperscalers.

The outlook for neoclouds like IREN is strong, with industry research projecting neoclouds like IREN to grow at a 69% CAGR. IREN is growing much faster than that. In the event that demand falls or IREN fails to execute on its action plans, IREN’s shares will be significantly impacted.

Takeaway

I believe IREN’s shares continue to look interesting despite the short hiccup that its Q2 FY26 ER presented last week. At 13x forward revenues, IREN’s shares look very appealing in terms of the risk/reward, leading me to reiterate my bullish views on IREN’s shares.

data.title: Market Sector Review: Extreme Market Bifurcation
data.description:
Since the beginning of the year, we have discussed the “reflation trade” and its impact on specific market sectors. This past weekend’s newsletter also showed some of these more extreme returns in various market sectors since the beginning of the year. To wit:

“Despite what seemed like a rough week in the market, it really wasn’t as most sectors and markets, outside of technology, moved into very overbought territory on a short-term basis. Energy, Materials, Industrials, and Staples, or rather “value,” have strongly outperformed every other market and sector this year. These areas are the most overbought, and a rotation back to growth seems increasingly obvious.”

There are several key takeaways from the analysis below.

  1. Since the beginning of 2026, Staples (up 15%), Industrials (up 12%), Energy (up 21%), and Materials (up 17%) have vastly outperformed the market as a whole, which is effectively flat YTD.
  2. The performance differential of those sectors versus the markets, and the deviations from their 50-day moving averages, are at extremes.
  3. While the overall market has been trading weakly since January, that is only a function of the largest market sectors, by capitalization, underperforming and are now oversold.

As shown below, those market sectors make up a relatively small portion of the overall index: Basic Materials (1.93%), Industrials (8.26%), Energy (3.11%), and Staples (5.76%). In other words, those 4 sectors combined (~19%) are smaller than the Technology sector alone (~29%). This also suggests that the relative outperformance of those sectors in recent weeks has more than offset the weakness in the Technology sector.

The breadth of those market sectors has also been extremely strong, with very high percentages of stocks in those sectors trading above their respective 50-, 100-, and 200-day moving averages, versus very low percentages in Communications, Technology, and Financials.

While the current outperformance of those select market sectors has been very enticing this year, the risk to investors comes if the “Reflation Narrative” falters for any reason. Those risks could come from a stronger dollar, falling inflation, or the realization that the overvaluation of these sectors has reached levels more historically extreme, given that these sectors have historically had some of the weakest revenue growth, particularly Energy.

With that said, since today is “Presidents’ Day” and the markets are closed, we will go through a technical review of each S&P 500 market sector, with a brief trading setup heading into next week.

How To Read The Charts

Using the S&P 500 Index as an example, this brief tutorial explains how to read the charts.

  1. The red line is the daily price for the past 3 years.
  2. The shaded areas are Bollinger Bands, which track the price’s standard deviation relative to the underlying moving average. In our examples, we are measuring price at 2 and 3 standard deviations from the 50-day moving average.
  3. The green line is the 200-day moving average.
  4. The shaded grey area in the background is a Williams %R reading, which measures short-term overbought and oversold conditions.
  5. The bottom graph is a Relative Strength Index, which also measures overbought and oversold conditions.

Okay, let’s get to it.

Sector Review

Basic Materials

  • Basic Materials broke through previous resistance and is now 3 standard deviations above the moving average. However, the trend remains bullish.
  • Currently, the price is very deviated above the rising 200-day, and extension risk is high after a steep run into 53 to 54.
  • With both longer-term readings at more extreme overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-dma, which is now critical support.
  • Action plan: Trim into strength, raise stops under 52 for tactical positions. Longer-term trend stop is near 47 to 48. Avoid new buys until 52 or 50 holds.

Staples

  • Like XLB, Staples has also surged through previous resistance and is now well into 3 standard deviations above the moving average.
  • Currently, the price is very deviated above the rising 200-day, and extension risk is high after a steep run into 88 to 90.
  • With both longer-term readings at more extreme overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-dma, which is now critical support and sits near the previous breakout levels.
  • Action plan: Trim into strength, raise stops under 82 for tactical positions. Longer-term trend stop is near 79 to 80. Avoid new buys until a correction holds at either previous breakout levels or the 200-day moving average.

Industrials

  • The chase also continues in Industrials, which is also 3 standard deviations above its mean. If you review the chart carefully, you will see that while these deviations can persist for some time, they eventually revert.
  • Currently, the price is very deviated above the rising 200-day, and extension risk is high after a steep run into 173 to 174.
  • With both longer-term readings at more extreme overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-dma, but critical support remains at the previous breakout levels at 155.
  • Action plan: Trim into strength, raise stops under 163 for tactical positions. Longer-term trend stop is near 153. Avoid new buys until a correction holds at either previous breakout levels or the 200-day moving average.

Financials

  • Unlike our previous market sectors, the Financials sector has been under considerable pressure. XLF is now 3 standard deviations below its mean, and is at levels that have previously constituted buying opportunities.
  • However, the break below the 200-day moving average does provide near-term risk. Hold off on purchases until the moving average is recovered.
  • With both longer-term readings nearing oversold levels, the risk of a deeper correction is subsiding.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 50, which is critical support from previous lows.
  • Action plan: Keep a watch on financials for a recovery that reclaims and holds the 200-day moving average. Keep stops very close to current levels.

Consumer Discretionary

  • Like XLF, the Discretionary sector has also been under considerable pressure. XLY has broken through its 50-day moving average and is approaching critical support at its 200-day moving average. Unlike XLF, XLY is not nearly as oversold, so the sector currently has more downside risk. Remain cautious with positioning.
  • As noted, the 200-day moving average provides near-term support, and XLY needs to hold those levels to maintain its bullish trend.
  • Both longer-term readings are approaching oversold levels, but not there yet, which suggests downside risks persist.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 115, which is the 200-day moving average.
  • Action plan: Keep a watch on the discretionary to see if it can hold the 200-day moving average. Keep stops at that level for now until price action improves.

Healthcare

  • The Healthcare sector remains in more neutral territory. It is neither grossly extended nor oversold, which keeps positioning stable for now.
  • However, the deviation above the 200-day moving average is extreme, and the sector could be forming a top at current levels.
  • With RSI at a more neutral level, keep holdings at current target weights but be mindful of risks.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 150, which is the previous sell-off low.
  • Action plan: Hold healthcare positions for now, but maintain around 150. If a rotation out of healthcare begins, it is a decent drop to the 200-day moving average.

Transportation

  • Transportation stocks have been on a massive run since the “Liberation Day” lows last year. Transports recently peaked 3 standard deviations above its mean and appears to have started a short-term correction.
  • However, the deviation above the 200-day moving average remains significant, so tighten up stops and take profits in the sector if you have not already.
  • Both longer-term readings are starting to reverse from overbought levels, which potentially adds to current risk levels.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 95, the previous support level.
  • Action plan: Take profits and rebalance the sector back to target weights. Keep stops on trading positions at the 50-day moving average with an understanding there is risk down to the 200-day.

Technology

  • Technology has been the poster child of the selloff recently. Notably, the technology sector is trying to hold support at lows from last November. A failure to do so would imply potentially more downside risk.
  • Notably, the 200-day moving average is a strong support level for both traders and investors.
  • With both longer-term readings nearing oversold levels, the risk of a deeper correction is subsiding.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 135, which is the 200-day moving average.
  • Action plan: If you failed to take profits previously in technology, it is a little late to do that now. Hold current positions with a stop at the 200-day moving average. Look to add to holdings if the sector can begin to improve relative strength, reclaim the 50-day moving average, and start to show an improvement in money flows.

Utilities

  • As we discussed last year, the Utilities sector was hated, and no one wanted to own the “boring” stuff. That tone has changed this year, with XLU surging through resistance and pushing well above the 3-standard-deviation level.
  • However, the recent breakout to new highs for XLU is encouraging and could foster further buying. However, the extension does pose a trading risk.
  • With both longer-term readings overbought, higher prices are likely to become more problematic.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at the 200-day moving average, which is key support.
  • Action plan: Utilities are caught up in the reflation narrative, but the fundamentals don’t support the speculation. Hold positions but keep stops at the 200-day moving average.

Real Estate

  • Like XLU, Real Estate has also seen a sudden surge in buying interest, and money rotates from growth to value. However, a lot of the stocks in these sectors are trading more like growth than value. The extreme deviation above the mean in XLRE, like XLU, is a warning, but it can persist for some time. So hold positions but manage your risk.
  • However, the 200-day moving average is nearby and provides a useful benchmark for both traders and investors to manage their risk exposures.
  • With both longer-term readings nearing overbought levels, the risk of a correction is rising.
  • Keep stops on trading positions at 41, which is the 200-day moving average for now.
  • Action plan: Keep a watch on real estate for buyer exhaustion as the run is getting extended. Hold positions for now, but taking profits and rebalancing to target weights never hurts. The 200-day moving average remains key support.

Conclusion

The current bifurcation inside the S&P 500 raises investor risk as price leadership has shifted toward sectors with weaker fundamental momentum, as noted above. Energy, Utilities, Industrials, and Materials work best when inflation rises, rates rise, and growth stays steady enough to support pricing power. That is the “reflation trade” narrative, but recent economic reports are undermining it as growth slows, inflation and wages fall, and rates decline.

Furthermore, given that markets eventually trade on revenue and earnings growth rates, which foster valuation premiums, the Technology, Communications, and parts of Discretionary are seeing significant discounts to expected growth rates. As such, when capital crowds into slow-growth defensives and cyclical inflation hedges while selling the higher-growth complex, the index loses internal quality. As we have seen, breadth narrows, volatility rises, broad market rallies shorten, and drawdowns get faster once the rotation exhausts.

This setup also increases policy risk. If inflation data continues to cool or the market reprices the path of rates lower, the reflation trade tends to unwind quickly. Energy and Materials face demand risk; Industrials face order risk; and Utilities are exposed to rate sensitivity, especially if yields fall quickly after a risk event. At the same time, the growth sectors being sold often have the strongest balance sheets and the highest margins, so when selling pressure hits those areas, it often reflects de-risking rather than a negative impact on future growth.

If you are overexposed to the reflation narrative, consider taking some gains and reducing concentration. Tightening risk controls, rebalancing toward target weights, and starting to focus on quality growth and cash flow durability is a good start. If you are overexposed to growth, focus on trimming the weakest balance sheets first, then add back only after price stabilizes and leadership improves. The goal is balance, not a single macro bet.

Trade accordingly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nebius Group Q4 Earnings: My CliffsNotes On Revenue Recognition
data.description:
Nebius Group Trades at 800+ P/E With FQ4 Earnings

Nebius Group, Inc. (NBIS) recently released their earnings report (ER) on Feb 12, 2026. As a battlefront AI infrastructure stock, many aspects of the ER have been quickly dissected by many other analysts. As such, in this article, I will focus on 3 aspects that are less discussed yet important to the understanding of high-growth stocks: negative accounting EPS, revenue recognition, and surging CAPEX. My goals are to explain A) why the extreme headline P/E ratios can be very misleading because of the difference between accounting EPS and cash flow and B) why the record CAPEX is more concerning in comparison with the EPS and/or P/E ratios, especially when combined with the company’s balance sheet changes.

Let me start with the issues of its EPS and P/E ratios. With the FQ4 ER, NBIS reported a quarterly loss of $0.99 in terms of GAAP EPS, leading to a TTM P/E ratio of 855x on a GAAP basis, as seen in the next chart below. The EPS is negative on non-GAAP, thus resulting in no meaningful P/E ratios. Next, I will examine these accounting EPS figures more closely and explain why they paint a very poor picture of Nebius’ true earnings.

NBIS: Disconnect Between Accounting EPS and True Cash Flow

In my view, the reconciliation section of the cash flow statement is where the magic or the mess happens. As the section bridges the gap between the Accounting Net Income (which follows accrual rules) and the actual cash that hit the company's bank account. The essence of stock analysis is large to understand this gap in my experiences. If you share similar experiences, you can see why NBIS’ Q4 reconciliation caught my attention. Two items - Accounts Receivable (A/R) and Unearned Revenue (UR)—are particularly noteworthy, and they are shown in the screenshot below (note that I skipped several line items in the screenshot to minimize clustering). These items created the biggest divergence between the EPS you see in the headlines and the company's true profits in Q4, in my assessment.

Starting with A/R, it represents money that customers owe Nebius for services already delivered (like GPU compute time) but haven't paid for yet. This boosts Net Income and EPS, even if they haven't received a single cent in cash. As such, on the cash flow statement, an increase in A/R is subtracted from the Net Income as seen. And this is the second-largest reconciliation item in Q4, as seen at -$632 million.

Now moving on to the UR, this is the opposite of A/R in the sense that it’s prepayment cash Nebius has received from customers for services they haven't provided yet (e.g., a customer paying upfront for a year of reserved H100 capacity). Because the service hasn't been delivered, GAAP accounting rules do not allow this to be counted as earnings and therefore do not help the EPS. But it is cash received, and the cash flow statement recognizes this by an increase in UR and adds it back to Net Income. The change in UR is the largest reconciliation item in Q4, as seen at $1,566 million.

With these reconciliations, the company’s operating cash flow is actually not only positive but also quite sizable for the quarter at $834 million.

Nebius Stock: CAPEX Surge

Understanding the above reconciliation practice also helps me to better contextualize NBIS’ CAPEX expansion plan. As background, the company spent about $955 million in Q3 on capital projects, as seen in the next chart below. The figure surged to more than $2 billion in Q4. Given its expansion plan announced in the ER (see more details quoted below), I expect elevated CAPEX to persist in the near future given its goal to reach 1 GW of connected power and a large data center in France.

Seeking Alpha News: CEO Volozh noted that in the fourth quarter of 2025, capital expenditures were about $2.1B, mainly driven by purchases of GPUs and GPU-related hardware and its data center expansion activities, including investments in planned greenfield sites. The company noted that it remains on track to reach 800 MW to 1 GW of connected power by the end of 2026. The company now expects to have more than 3 GW of contracted power by year-end. Nebius is planning a new 240 MW data center in Béthune, France, that will be one of Europe’s largest when it is finished.

The Q4 CAPEX surge could seem extreme. For example, it exceeds the total revenue the company reported in the quarter (of around $227 million) by almost 10-fold. However, with an understanding of the differences between accounting earnings and true cash flow, the picture is far less extreme—but still quite stretched, though. Q4’s CAPEX of around $2B is about 2.4x the operating cash flow of $384 million.

Going forward, the picture could further improve as the company’s Annual Recurring Revenue (or ARR) grows. For example, with the Q4 ER, the company’s ARR as of year-end was $1.25B, ahead of its most recent guidance in the range of $900M to $1.1B. As the company keeps growing its AI infrastructure business, the impacts of revenue recognition could become even larger and more crucial for investors to understand its true economic earnings and CAPEX sustainability.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

As just mentioned, the CAPEX surge is far less extreme than what appears on the surface, but it is still quite stretched in my view and exceeds the organic cash flow by a wide margin. As a result, the company has been relying on debt financing, whose role has been increasing at a rapid pace and warrants close monitoring. Per its Q4 balance sheet as shown below, its debt has expanded dramatically to support its pivot into AI infrastructure. At the end of Q4, total liabilities reached approximately $7.8 billion. In contrast, the reading was almost negligible ($295 million) as reported a year ago and was “only” $5.3 billion even a quarter ago. This growth is primarily driven by the surge in long-term debt, which stands at $4.1 billion now. The company had no long-term debt till the June quarter of 2025. These obligations, combined with the cash flow deficit just mentioned, could impact its CAPEX sustainability as the company scales.

To close, this NBIS analysis focused on its fourth quarter 2025 ER with an emphasis on 3 items less discussed: negative GAAP EPS, revenue recognition, and surging CAPEX. An examination of these items has led me to see a wide gap between its GAAP EPS and true cash flows and believe the CAPEX surge is less concerning than on the surface. As the company keeps growing its ARR at rapid rates, I recommend investors pay close attention to how the company adjusts its future revenues (and manages its balance sheet too).

data.title: Midstream MLPs: High Income With Equity Characteristics
data.description:
Master Limited Partnerships in the energy midstream sector have long appealed to income investors because of their ability to generate steady, fee-based cash flows from transporting oil and gas. Unlike upstream producers, pipeline operators are largely insulated from commodity price volatility, relying instead on contracted volumes and long-lived infrastructure assets. That stability has historically supported elevated distributions relative to many other income vehicles.¹

In recent years, the financial profile of the midstream sector has improved meaningfully. Balance sheets have strengthened, leverage has declined, and distribution coverage has become more conservative. These changes have helped make payouts more durable than they were during prior energy cycles. Still, MLPs remain equity securities. Their distributions are not contractual obligations and can be adjusted if business conditions deteriorate. Unit prices also fluctuate with broader equity markets and shifts in energy sentiment.

The distinguishing advantage of MLPs is flexibility. As infrastructure utilization rises and cash flows expand, distributions can grow over time. That growth potential differentiates midstream assets from fixed-income securities, whose payments are static by design.² For investors willing to tolerate equity risk, MLPs offer a combination of income and long-term cash-flow participation that traditional bonds cannot replicate.

Preferred Stocks and Bonds: Stability With Trade-Offs

Preferred stocks occupy a middle ground between bonds and common equity. They typically pay fixed dividends and rank above common shares in the capital structure but below bonds. Recent interest rate volatility pushed preferred prices lower, lifting yields across much of the market. That higher income comes with limitations. Preferred dividends can be suspended without triggering default, and holders have limited upside and no voting rights.³

REIT preferreds add another layer of complexity. Many issues are cumulative, meaning missed dividends must be paid before common distributions resume. However, because REITs do not pay corporate income taxes, their preferred dividends are generally taxed as ordinary income rather than qualifying for reduced dividend rates. This distinction can materially affect after-tax income depending on an investor’s tax situation.⁴

Corporate bonds remain the most traditional income option. Investment-grade bonds provide predictable cash flows and principal repayment at maturity, offering stability that appeals to conservative investors. Their yields tend to be lower than those of MLPs or preferreds, reflecting their higher claim on assets and contractual payment structure. High-yield bonds narrow the income gap but introduce credit risk and limited upside.⁵

Yield, Risk, and Taxes: Choosing the Right Mix

Taxes are a critical differentiator across income strategies. A substantial portion of MLP distributions is typically classified as return of capital, allowing taxes to be deferred until units are sold. This feature can significantly enhance after-tax income for long-term holders, though it adds complexity through K-1 filings and potential multi-state tax exposure.⁶ Preferred dividends from taxable corporations may qualify for favorable tax treatment, while bond interest is fully taxable unless held in tax-advantaged accounts.

Ultimately, the choice between midstream MLPs, preferred stocks, and corporate bonds depends on an investor’s objectives and risk tolerance. Bonds offer predictability and capital stability. Preferreds provide incremental yield with moderate structural risk. MLPs deliver higher income potential and tax efficiency, balanced by equity market exposure and sector-specific risks.

Rather than viewing these options as substitutes, many income-focused portfolios blend them. Diversifying across contractual income, hybrid securities, and equity-based cash flows can help smooth results across market environments while preserving flexibility as conditions evolve.

Footnotes

  1. Meketa Investment Group, Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs) (June 2022).

  2. Hennessy Funds, “What’s Driving Midstream Company Performance?” (January 2025).

  3. Charles Schwab, “Are Preferred Securities Still Attractive?” (January 28, 2025).

  4. Innovative Income Investor, “Preferred Stocks of REITs” (December 28, 2025).

  5. Charles Schwab, “Corporate Bonds and Portfolio Stability” (2025).

  6. ETF Database (VettaFi), “MLP Distributions Versus Corporate Dividends” (August 11, 2022).

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA’s appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA’s trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. data.title: OFS Credit: Stick To The Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

OFS Credit (OCCI) is an externally managed closed-end fund, focusing on CLO equity and debt. While the CEF does contain some CLO debt, the very vast majority of the assets consists of CLO equity, as OFS Credit focuses on the higher-yielding securities. The weighted average effective yield of the investments was approximately 13%.

A look at the full-year results

During its financial year, OFS Credit had to navigate a tougher credit environment as Morningstar reported the total default rates doubled from 0.73% to 1.46%, backing off from a multi-year high at 1.50% at the end of the third quarter in the calendar year 2025. Meanwhile, the CLO market is still booming as the total amount of private credit offerings increased by a high single digit percentage during the year.

Looking at the FY 2025 results, we see OFS reported an interest income of $44.2M, while the total amount of operating expenses came in at almost $22M. Approximately 1/3 rd of the total operating expenses are related to the interest expenses, with the combination of incentive fees and base management fees making up about half of the expenses.

This resulted in a net investment income of $22.6M, and divided over the weighted average share count throughout the year, this represented a NII/share of around 88 cents. A good result, but the statement of operations also shows there was a $3.6M realized loss, while the total fair value of its portfolio decreased by almost $29M.

As OFS Credit is a ‘serial ATM issuer,’ its share count continued to increase throughout the year. At the end of October, OFS had just over 28M shares outstanding, resulting in a NAV/share of $5.46.

That's, of course, almost four months ago, and in a more recent update, OFS Credit has disclosed the current NAV/share is approximately $4.31-4.41.

At the end of October, the balance sheet contained $271M in assets, backed by $114M in preferred stock. As these are term preferred shares with mandatory redemption dates, the preferred shares are listed as a liability rather than an equity component.

What does this mean for the fixed income securities?

I rarely invest in the common equity of CLO equity-focused CEFs. I’d rather invest in a more senior security and accept lower distributions versus running the risks associated with being first in line in case things go south. Additionally, OFS Credit’s continuous share printing program erodes the value of the common shares.

OFS Credit has five series of preferred shares outstanding, shown below.

Three of these issues are currently listed. The 6.125% Series C term preferred is trading with OCCIO as its ticker symbol and currently trades around par. I’d say this is the lowest-risk security, as the mandatory redemption date is at the end of April. As this security pays its preferred dividend in monthly installments, you could look at OCCIO as some sort of ‘term deposit,’ collecting a 1% return in dividend payments. While there are no guarantees, I don’t expect to see any major issues that would prohibit or delay the repayment of these securities.

I’m also keeping an eye on the Series E preferred stock, trading with OCCIN as its ticker symbol. This issue has a lower preferred dividend coupon of just 5.25%, but matures at the end of this year. At the current share price of $24.56, this would result in a capital gain of 1.8% in the next ten months.

Investment thesis

While I’m personally not interested in initiating a long position in the common units issued by OFS Credit, I do acknowledge certain investors could be interested in the high yield, but the NAV erosion just goes too fast for my liking. Additionally, as interest rates may move down a bit more in the near future, the default risk should decrease (although this will likely go hand in hand with lower returns on the investments).

I’m focusing on the senior securities issued by OFS Credit, as I want the additional layer of safety. Although OCCIO will be retired in just over two months, it’s an interesting vehicle to park some cash (subject to reasonable transaction expenses, of course).

I currently have no position in any of OFS’ preferred stock, but I certainly would consider a long position when a good opportunity arises.
